title: Web Security: SQL Injection and Browser Attacks
description: Practice how attackers abuse trust boundaries in web apps, then defend them with parameterization, encoding, and session controls.

content.value: A web application becomes vulnerable when it treats untrusted user input as trusted instructions. In this module, you will inspect login flows, reason about attacker decisions, and practice safe fixes through short checks and a simulated lab.

content.alt: Web request flow showing browser input, backend validation, and database query boundary
content.caption: The dangerous moment is the boundary where user-controlled data enters code, queries, or rendered HTML.

content.title: Input Trust Boundary
steps: Output is encoded for the browser context

content.question: A login query is built by concatenating the email and password directly into a SQL string. What is the most important fix?
options: Use parameterized queries for email and password
Increase the password minimum length only
content.answer: Use parameterized queries for email and password
content.explanation: Parameterized queries keep attacker-controlled input as data, so it cannot rewrite the SQL logic.

content.title: Incident Triage
content.situation: Support reports that several accounts were accessed without password reset emails. Logs show repeated login attempts containing quote characters and SQL comments.

decisions.label: Block the source IP only
decisions.feedback: This may slow one attacker, but it does not fix the vulnerable query.

decisions.label: Patch the login query with parameters and review logs for affected accounts
decisions.feedback: Best response: fix the root cause and scope the incident.

decisions.label: Disable all user accounts permanently
decisions.feedback: This is disruptive and still does not identify or fix the flaw.

content.title: SQL Injection Login Bypass
content.instructions: This is a safe simulation. Try an input that would comment out the password condition in a vulnerable login query.
content.successMessage: Login bypass successful. In a real system, parameterized queries prevent this payload from changing query logic.
content.failureMessage: The simulated login still checks the password. Try thinking about how SQL comments can remove the rest of a WHERE clause.

content.question: Which browser defense reduces the impact of a stolen XSS payload by restricting allowed script sources?
content.explanation: CSP can restrict where scripts may load from and whether inline scripts can execute.

content.title: What to remember
bullets: Treat all browser input as untrusted.
Use parameterized queries for SQL values.
Encode output for its exact browser context.
Use logs and incident review to validate the real blast radius.

quiz.question: Which SQL injection mitigation provides code/data separation?
